09fbf284-95c2-45dd-ab2f-1aeab21a2c24.titulo: Who the Producer is and the types of production
09fbf284-95c2-45dd-ab2f-1aeab21a2c24.conteudo_rico:
To start: Before spreadsheets and riders, you need to understand WHO has the final say within a production and why.

The producer is the backbone of an event production. In this lesson you will understand the different production roles, where each one begins and ends, and why the executive producer is the loudest voice in the operation. Understanding the hierarchy avoids the classic beginner mistake: trying to do everything alone or stepping on a colleague's role.

  • Executive / General Producer: Responsible for ALL layers of the team: transportation, lodging, payments, approvals, and the coordination of the other producers (technical and artistic). This is the head of the operation and the final voice in any decision. When there is conflicting information between departments, this person decides. The entire chain of financial and contractual responsibility runs through them.
  • Artistic Producer: Coordinates and manages the aspects of artistic creation: selecting musicians, choosing the repertoire, organizing rehearsals and recordings, supervising the musical production, and defining the creative direction. Ensures that the project's artistic vision is realized in the best possible way. In shows, they align the set, the transitions, and the key moments of the performance.
  • Technical Producer: Takes care of the technical crew. Reviews the details of the rider with the client, validates what the venue offers, organizes transportation and lodging for the technical crew (hired by the general producer), and rents or manages the necessary equipment. This is the bridge between the artist's technical requirements and the reality of the venue.
  • Personal Producer (of the artist): Focuses on the administrative side and the day-to-day logistical coordination of the artist. Unlike the artistic or executive producer, this person takes care of the artist's personal needs, ensuring they have all the support to focus on their career. Handles the personal schedule, travel, meals, and well-being.
  • Why hierarchy matters: In an event operation, decisions need an owner. If 'everyone decides,' no one decides, and delays or mistakes happen. Clearly defining who is responsible for each layer (and who has the final word) is what distinguishes a professional production from a costly amateur job.

Equipment / details

  • Production org chart: A diagram that shows the hierarchy and who reports to whom. It lets the whole team know who to report each type of problem to. Build a simple one in Google Slides or Canva and post it in the event group.
  • Team contact list (spreadsheet): A spreadsheet with the name, role, phone/WhatsApp, and responsibility of each member. It is the first document the producer builds. Always keep it updated.

Step by step

  1. 1. Define the project's layers: List which fronts your event has: artistic, technical, logistical, financial, security. Not every event has all of them.
  2. 2. Assign a person in charge per layer: For each front, define who is responsible. In small events, one person may take on multiple roles; record this explicitly.
  3. 3. Establish the final voice: Make it clear, in writing in the group, that the executive producer decides in case of a deadlock. This prevents gridlock when the pressure is on.
  4. 4. Draw and share the org chart: Distribute the diagram to the entire team before the event.

In practice: Picture a mid-sized show. The client calls complaining about the load-in/load-out schedule. Who solves it? The technical producer talks to the crew and the venue; but the decision to pay for an extra hour of dock time is financial, so it goes through the executive producer. Knowing this flow in seconds is what keeps the event moving.

Summary

  • The executive producer coordinates all layers and has the final word.
  • The artistic producer handles creation; the technical producer handles the crew and the rider; the personal producer handles the artist.
  • Defining who is responsible and the final voice prevents gridlock under pressure.

Who is the Event Producer?

The event producer is the professional responsible for planning, coordinating, and executing every stage of an event — from the initial briefing to the final accounting. Depending on the scale and nature of the project, this role splits into complementary profiles: the Executive Producer leads strategically and handles contracts; the Technical Producer masters the audio, video, and structure infrastructure; the Creative Producer develops concept and experience; and the Operations Coordinator ensures execution on the day of the event. The types of production — institutional, corporate, shows, social, sports, and hybrid — determine the context, the client, and the specific requirements of each project, shaping the profile and competencies the producer needs to develop.

101dcafb-40db-4f1e-b6b1-fdb2358ecb97.conteudo_rico:
To start: Tools can be learned; posture is built. What separates a reliable producer from an improviser is the set of behavioral competencies.

No spreadsheet saves a production run by someone disorganized or who fails to communicate. This lesson consolidates the producer's core competencies and shows how each one translates into practical action in daily work.

  • Proactivity: Taking the initiative and acting ahead of time, identifying problems and opportunities before they become evident. The proactive producer does not wait for the problem to blow up: they anticipate needs, plan in advance, and act to influence the outcome. It is the competency that prevents most fires.
  • Discernment: The ability to analyze and assess situations clearly and sensibly to make prudent decisions. It involves distinguishing options, understanding consequences, and choosing the best approach with rational and ethical criteria. Essential in ambiguous situations where there is no obvious answer.
  • Organization: Structuring, planning, and managing tasks, resources, and information efficiently. Prioritizing tasks, setting clear goals, tracking deadlines, and keeping everything accessible. This includes keeping the schedule always updated and confirming new dates and times with the team.
  • Communication: Expressing ideas clearly and listening well, adapting the message to the audience and context. Effective communication builds relationships, enables collaboration, and resolves conflicts. At an event, poor communication turns into delays and rework.
  • Reliability: Being someone the team can depend on: consistent in actions and words, keeping promises, ethical, and discreet. Trust allows people to share information and work together. It is built over time and is the foundation of networking in the industry.
  • Focus: Keeping attention on the event's goal even amid noise, the unexpected, and pressure. The producer who loses focus loses control of the schedule.

Equipment / details

  • Digital calendar (Google Calendar): Keeps dates, times, and team confirmations always updated. Use reminders and invites with RSVP to record who confirmed.
  • Task app / checklist: Trello, Notion, or Google Keep to record pending items and not rely solely on memory. It turns proactivity into a system.

Step by step

  1. 1. Do a self-assessment: For each competency, rate yourself from 1 to 5. Be honest.
  2. 2. Attack the weakest competency: Choose the lowest-rated one and define a concrete action (e.g., organization → start using a task spreadsheet today).
  3. 3. Create confirmation routines: Every new date/time must be confirmed with the team in writing. Make it a habit.

In practice: A supplier warns that the truck will be 2 hours late. The proactive producer already had the phone number of a backup supplier (organization), assesses whether it is worth waiting or switching (discernment), notifies the team immediately (communication), and stays calm so as not to contaminate the team (focus). The five competencies act together in seconds.

Summary

  • Proactivity, discernment, organization, communication, and reliability are the producer's core.
  • Competencies translate into routines: an updated calendar, written confirmations, a plan B ready.
  • Under pressure, the competencies act together and in seconds.

Image: Production Org Chart — who reports to whom: Executive Producer → Technical Production · Stage Coordinator · Logistics / Transport · Finance / Admin. The technical areas (Sound, Light, Video, Effects, Structure, Safety) report to Technical Production and the Stage Coordinator.

Competencies of the Professional Event Producer

The professional event producer is a multidisciplinary manager who combines planning, communication, technical mastery, financial control, logistics, and resilience under pressure. Their profile is not defined by a single skill, but by the balance between hard competencies (budgeting, A/V technical specification, contracts) and soft competencies (leadership, conflict management, client communication). Knowing your current level in each dimension is the first step toward intentional professional development. This simulator lets the student map their profile, identify gaps, and test their decision-making in real event-market situations.

29163c42-4b0f-44d9-89bb-6d434e22c9ab.titulo: Briefing: the document that gives rise to the event
29163c42-4b0f-44d9-89bb-6d434e22c9ab.conteudo_rico:
To start: Every good event is born from a clear briefing. Without it, the production becomes expensive guesswork.

The briefing is the document that provides the essential information and guidelines to steer the creation and execution of a project. It aligns all involved parties around a shared understanding of the goals and expectations. In this lesson you will learn the complete structure of a briefing and how to run it with the client.

  • General information: Project title, start date and expected completion date, and the location where the event will be held. It is the header that identifies the project.
  • Project objective: A description of what the event is, its purpose, and what it is expected to achieve. It includes the target audience: who the event is made for. Everything that comes after must serve this objective.
  • Inspiration and concept: The theme or concept that guides the production and the visual references (images, works, examples) that inspire the creation. It gives the event its 'face.'
  • Format and media: The type of event/art (show, play, exhibition, performance) and the specific tools or materials to be used.
  • Budget: The total budget available, including materials, equipment, fees, and other costs. It is the limit that defines what is possible.
  • Schedule and logistics: Key dates (milestones, rehearsals, setup, opening), characteristics of the space, and technical requirements (lighting, sound, projection).
  • Team, approvals, and metrics: A list of participants and roles, the process and number of approvals/revisions, success metrics (how the result will be measured), feedback management, and the contacts along with the frequency of updates.

Equipment / details

  • Briefing template (Google Docs/Forms): A standardized document with all the fields above. Use a Google Form for the client to fill out and feed directly into a spreadsheet, or a shared Doc to fill out during the meeting.
  • Visual reference folder: A Drive folder or a Pinterest/Notion board to gather the concept references the client brings.

Step by step

  1. 1. Schedule the briefing meeting: Set it up with the client/contractor. Bring the ready template to fill out field by field.
  2. 2. Fill in general information and objective: Start with the name, date, location, and, most importantly, the objective and target audience.
  3. 3. Capture concept and references: Ask for visual examples. 'Show me an event you admire' unlocks the concept.
  4. 4. Lock the budget and key dates: Without the budget ceiling and the milestones, nothing else can be planned.
  5. 5. Define approvals and success metrics: Who approves each stage and how success will be measured (attendance, satisfaction, impact).
  6. 6. Validate and distribute: Reread it with the client, adjust, and share the final version with the team. Clarity and precision are essential.

In practice: A client says: 'I want an unforgettable show.' Unusable. With the briefing you turn that into: an event on 09/20, for 800 people aged 25-40, an '80s concept, an R$80,000 budget, success measured by turnout and a satisfaction survey. Now it can be produced.

Summary

  • The briefing aligns everyone around a shared understanding of the project.
  • Without an objective, audience, budget, and key dates, there is no planning.
  • Clarity and precision in the briefing prevent rework and surprises.

Image: The Producer's Documents — what each one solves: Briefing · Budget · Technical Rider · Work Order · Stage Plan · Schedule.

Briefing: the event's birth certificate

The briefing is the document that formalizes everything the producer knows — and what still needs to be discovered — about an event. It is born from active listening with the client and gathers objective, audience, structure, budget, and risks in a single reference point for the whole team.

A well-made briefing prevents rework, expectation conflicts, and surprises during execution. Fields such as the event objective, the number of pax, and mandatory licenses are critical: without them, suppliers cannot quote, and the production starts without a foundation.

In this simulator you build a real briefing in 4 phases — identification, audience, technical structure, and risks — and learn which pieces of information block the document's approval and why each of them matters in practice.

09b1d99a-1337-4c67-85f7-c108e7c993e8.titulo: Licenses, authorizations, and legal documentation for the event
09b1d99a-1337-4c67-85f7-c108e7c993e8.conteudo_rico:
To start: An event without its paperwork in order is a fine (or a shutdown) waiting to happen.

Licenses vary according to the type of event, its scale, and the municipality/state. The producer does not need to be a lawyer, but does need to know WHICH fronts exist and to engage legal counsel and the right agencies. IMPORTANT: requirements, deadlines, and agencies change according to local legislation; always confirm with the city hall and with specialized counsel.

  • Event licenses: City hall authorization permit (use of the space), Fire Department license (protection against fire and panic), environmental license (when there is an impact, such as open-air shows), operating license for a temporary event, authorization to use amplified sound, and authorization to sell food and beverages (sanitary surveillance).
  • Copyright (ECAD): To perform protected music, authorization/payment to ECAD is required, ensuring compensation for authors and composers. It applies to both live and recorded music.
  • Specific cases: License to sell tickets (paid event), license for fireworks, and a safety/fire-protection license for enclosed venues. Each has its own agency and requirement.
  • Venue documentation: Occupancy permit (venue fit for use), structural safety report (stages, bleachers), sanitary permit (food/restrooms), and the venue rental contract.
  • Hiring documentation: Service contracts, the tax IDs (CNPJ/CPF) of the providers, qualification certificates (operators, security guards, fire wardens), and civil liability insurance to cover accidents.
  • Prior notice to the authorities: Military Police (public safety), Traffic Department (impact on traffic), and Child Protective Council (events with minors). Giving advance notice prevents problems on the day.
  • Civil liability insurance: It is not a license, but it covers damages or injuries during the event. Highly recommended for any event with an audience.

Equipment / details

  • License checklist per event: A spreadsheet where each license becomes a row with a status (to request / requested / issued), agency, deadline, and person responsible. It is the map of compliance.
  • 06_Legal_Documentation folder (Drive): A dedicated subfolder to store permits, reports, contracts, and receipts. Restricted access.
  • Specialized legal counsel: A professional or firm that validates contracts and licenses. For large events, it is an investment, not a cost.

Step by step

  1. 1. Map the local requirements: Consult the city hall of the event's municipality and/or legal counsel. The rules are local.
  2. 2. List all applicable licenses: Build the checklist marking what applies to your case (sound, food, fireworks, tickets, etc.).
  3. 3. Identify deadlines and responsible parties: Each agency has a deadline. Fire Department and environmental licenses tend to take time; start early.
  4. 4. Gather the documentation of the venue and the providers: Occupancy permit, structural report, contracts, tax IDs, qualification certificates.
  5. 5. Make the prior notifications: Military Police, traffic, and Child Protective Council when applicable.
  6. 6. Take out civil liability insurance: Size it according to the scale and the audience.

In practice: An open-air show for 2,000 people with beverage sales. Mandatory fronts: city hall permit, Fire Department, environmental license (noise/open-air), sound authorization, beverage authorization (sanitary), ECAD, notice to the Military Police and traffic, and insurance. Forgetting a single one of them can shut down the event on the eve.

Summary

  • Licenses vary by type, scale, and local legislation: always confirm with the city hall and counsel.
  • ECAD, Fire Department, sanitary, environmental, and permit are the most common fronts.
  • Documentation of the venue and providers plus insurance complete the compliance.

Licenses, Authorizations, and Legal Documentation for the Event

Holding an event with legal certainty requires a set of licenses that varies according to the type, the venue, the audience, and the activities involved. The Municipal Permit, the Fire Department Inspection Certificate (AVCB), the Technical Responsibility Note (ART) for temporary structures, and the ECAD license for public musical performance are documents of a critical nature — without them, the event may be embargoed or those responsible may be held civilly and criminally liable. Higher-risk activities, such as pyrotechnics and high-power lasers, require specific authorizations from the Brazilian Army and technical reports, respectively. The full-fledged producer must map the entire documentation chain at least 60 days in advance, organizing deadlines and responsible parties for each item.

fb0b730f-f363-440d-8370-0843d4ff9e39.conteudo_rico:
To start: The logistics run-sheet is the document that ensures each thing is in the right place at the right time.

A show's logistics run-sheet ensures that all elements are in the correct place and at the correct time, contributing to a smooth and efficient production. In this lesson you will learn the complete structure of the run-sheet and how to build it.

  • General information and team: Event name, date, time, and location. The team involved, divided into production, technical (sound, lighting, stage), and security, with names and contacts.
  • Schedule of the day: Hourly sequence: setup and sound check; lighting and set design setup; band/artist check-in (with dressing rooms); full rehearsal; doors open (ticket checking and audience guidance); and the live show.
  • Required resources: Sound and lighting equipment, instruments, dressing rooms, security guards, and audience service staff. It is the list of what needs to exist at the venue.
  • Load-in and load-out logistics: Designated times for load-out and for post-event loading, the specific load-in/load-out location, and the person responsible for this logistics.
  • Safety and emergency: Emergency exit points, the location of the first-aid kit, and evacuation procedures. A non-negotiable item.
  • Approvals, contacts, and attachments: Approval process, number of revisions, contact list, update frequency, signatures, and attachments (venue maps, relevant contracts).

Equipment / details

  • Logistics run-sheet template (Google Docs): A document with the sections above. Reuse it as a template across events, adjusting dates and names.
  • Hourly schedule spreadsheet: Columns for time, activity, and person responsible. It shows the entire day at a glance (see example in the next lesson).
  • Venue map / floor plan: An image or floor plan of the space marking the stage, load-in/load-out, emergency exits, dressing rooms, and medical post.

Step by step

  1. 1. Fill in the header and team: Event, date, location, and the team by area with contacts.
  2. 2. Build the hourly schedule: From the start of setup to the end of the show, hour by hour, with a person responsible per block.
  3. 3. List resources and load-in/load-out: What needs to exist and when it enters/leaves the venue, with a person responsible.
  4. 4. Define the safety and emergency plan: Exits, first aid, and evacuation. Communicate it to the team.
  5. 5. Attach the map and contracts and collect approvals: Venue map, relevant contracts, and signatures from the parties.
  6. 6. Distribute and keep a single version: Everyone works from the SAME version. Dating the file avoids confusion.

In practice: Without a run-sheet, the backline truck arrives right when the doors open and jams everything. With the run-sheet, the load-in was at 07:30, the stage was set up by 12:00, the soundcheck ran at 13:00, and the audience entered at 18:00 without crossing paths with equipment. The run-sheet is what prevents the chaos of the day.

Summary

  • The logistics run-sheet puts each thing in the right place and at the right time.
  • It covers team, schedule, resources, load-in/load-out, safety, and attachments.
  • A single, dated version avoids confusion on the day of the event.

Event Logistics Run-Sheet

The logistics run-sheet is the living document that coordinates people, equipment, and time across all phases of an event: planning (D-7), on-site preparation (D-1), execution on the day, and post-event teardown. Each phase has critical items — such as the permit, the technical rider, and the sound check — which, if neglected, compromise the entire production. The correct sequence of the setup timeline (load-in → set design → lighting → audio → video → sound check) is fundamental to avoid rework, space conflicts, and delays to the opening time. The producer who masters the logistics run-sheet is able to anticipate bottlenecks, distribute resources, and respond to incidents without impacting the audience.

26fbb910-2570-4488-a426-fea6346108e3.titulo: Schedules for the technical, artistic, and travel teams
26fbb910-2570-4488-a426-fea6346108e3.conteudo_rico:
To start: The schedule is the clock of the event. Whoever masters the schedule masters the operation.

There are distinct schedules that fit together: the technical team's, the artistic team's, and the travel/tour one. In this lesson you learn to build each one and to synchronize them so that sound, lighting, artist, and audience converge on the same point.

  • Technical team schedule: For the day of the event: setup and testing of sound and lighting; check-in and reception of the artist (dressing rooms and specific requirements); rehearsal and final adjustments; the live performance with constant monitoring of sound and lighting; and the teardown and loading of equipment post-event. The technical team must maintain constant communication (radio) and have a contingency plan for technical failures.
  • Artistic team schedule: Arrival and preparation in the dressing rooms; full rehearsal and sound/lighting/set-design adjustments; free time and rest; dinner; costume and makeup check; pre-show preparation (repertoire review, alignment with the band); the performance; and interaction with fans/media (autographs, photos, interviews).
  • Travel/tour schedule: For travel: airport check-in and document verification; flight; transfer and hotel check-in; acclimatization time; team meeting (itinerary review and briefing); rehearsals and preparation. On show days during a tour, it integrates local exploration, meals, preparation, the show, interaction with fans, and post-show debriefing.
  • Synchronizing the schedules: The three must talk to each other: the technical setup has to be ready before the artistic rehearsal; the artist's check-in depends on the travel; the soundcheck needs the stage set up. The producer is the one who ensures the fit.
  • Schedule briefing meeting: When sharing the schedules, hold briefing meetings to ensure everyone knows their responsibilities and the workflow. A schedule without a briefing is ignored paper.

Equipment / details

  • Schedule spreadsheet (Google Sheets): Columns: Time | Activity | Person responsible. One tab per front (technical, artistic, travel). Colors by status make it easier to read.
  • Shared calendar (Google Calendar): Records rehearsals, shows, travel, and deadlines. Invites with confirmation document who is aware.
  • Two-way radio / WhatsApp group: Constant communication on the day. Radio for the technical operation; the group for alignments and records.

Step by step

  1. 1. Define the show time (anchor): Everything is calculated from the show time, working backward.
  2. 2. Allocate the technical setup: Reserve plenty of time for setup and testing of sound/lighting. A delay here brings down everything else.
  3. 3. Fit in the artist's check-in and rehearsal: The artist arrives after the setup; the rehearsal happens with the stage ready.
  4. 4. Position preparation, costume, and pre-show: Dinner, rest, costume/makeup, and repertoire alignment before going on stage.
  5. 5. Synchronize with the travel: If there is travel, the travel schedule must deliver the artist before the check-in.
  6. 6. Hold the briefing meeting: Present the schedule and confirm that everyone understood their responsibility.

In practice: Show at 21:00. Working backward: pre-show 19:30, costume 19:00, dinner 18:00, rehearsal 14:00-16:00, technical setup 12:00-15:00, load-in 07:30. If the artist's flight only lands at 18:00, there is a conflict with dinner; the producer moves up the dinner or the transport. It is the fit of the three schedules that makes the show happen.

Summary

  • There are technical, artistic, and travel schedules, and they must synchronize.
  • Build backward from the show time.
  • A schedule without a briefing meeting is ignored paper.

Image: Critical Path of the Production — what CANNOT be delayed: Book venue → Close suppliers → Setup → Soundcheck → Event (with Promotion in parallel). Tasks in RED are the critical path: if one slips, the event slips. Prioritize them.

Schedules for the Technical, Artistic, and Travel Teams

In event production, the schedule defines the window for each team: logistics leaves first (freight and check-in), followed by the technical team for setup and testing; the artistic team arrives closer to the event for rehearsals and soundcheck — always on D-1, never on the day of the show. Delays at any stage propagate conflicts into the following ones, making it mandatory to control sequences and slack. A well-structured schedule is the producer's main governance instrument to ensure that all teams are ready and synchronized at the moment of opening to the public.

2a1cb3d4-8a10-40d8-978a-2f18b82d612f.titulo: Technical rider: the artist's technical document
2a1cb3d4-8a10-40d8-978a-2f18b82d612f.conteudo_rico:
To start: The technical rider is the bridge between what the artist requires and what the venue offers. Reading it wrong is costly.

The technical rider describes the technical specifications needed for the production: equipment, stage, power, crew, and hospitality. It is prepared by the artist/team and shared with the venue's technical crew. The technical producer reviews the rider with the client and validates what the venue delivers. In this lesson you learn to read, review, and use a rider.

  • Equipment: Detailed specifications for sound, lighting, stage, and other equipment, with the brands and models preferred by the artist (PA, monitors, microphones, backline). It is the technical 'shopping list.'
  • Stage configuration: The stage dimensions and layout, the number of microphones, cabinets, and monitors, and the set-design and visual-element requirements. It defines what needs to fit and where.
  • Electrical requirements: The power needs of all equipment and the strategic power points. Electrical failure is one of the main causes of on-stage problems.
  • Technical crew: The number and roles of the professionals needed: sound engineer (PA and monitor), lighting operator, stage technicians, and backline. It defines who needs to be present.
  • Dressing rooms and lodging: Dressing-room needs (water, specific food) and the lodging requirements of the crew and the artist. It is the hospitality rider, part of the care for the artist.
  • Security and load logistics: Security requirements on stage and at the venue, emergency instructions, and load-in/load-out times/locations, with parking needs.
  • Technical rider vs. hospitality rider: The technical rider deals with equipment and stage; the hospitality rider deals with the dressing room, food, transportation, and comfort. They often come together, but the producer handles each front with the right supplier.

Equipment / details

  • Technical rider (artist's PDF): The source document sent by the artist. Always keep the latest dated version in the artist's folder.
  • Stage plan / stage map: A drawing showing where each musician, instrument, monitor, and microphone goes on stage. It accompanies the rider and guides the setup.
  • Input list (channel list): A numbered list of each input on the sound console (channel 1: kick drum, channel 2: snare, etc.). It is what the sound engineer uses to hook everything up.
  • Rider check spreadsheet: A column for what the rider requests vs. what the venue offers vs. status (ok / missing / substitute). It is how the technical producer validates the rider.

Step by step

  1. 1. Receive and read the full rider: Do not skip. Every line of equipment and power matters.
  2. 2. Compare the rider vs. the venue's infrastructure: Build the check spreadsheet: what the rider requests and what the venue actually has.
  3. 3. Resolve the gaps: What is missing: rent it, substitute it with an equivalent (with the artist's approval), or negotiate. Document each decision.
  4. 4. Validate the stage plan and the input list: Review the stage map and the channel list with the sound engineer.
  5. 5. Handle hospitality separately: Dressing room, food, and lodging go to the right suppliers.
  6. 6. Confirm with the client and the venue's technical crew: Align expectations before the day. Fulfilling the rider is what guarantees the performance.

In practice: The rider requests an L-Acoustics PA, 6 in-ear monitor mixes, SM58s on the vocals, and a standard drum kit. The venue only has 4 monitor mixes. The technical producer identifies the gap in the spreadsheet, rents 2 extra mixes, and confirms with the artist's engineer before the soundcheck. Without this review, the problem would only appear at show time, with no solution.

Summary

  • The technical rider specifies equipment, stage, power, crew, and hospitality.
  • The technical producer checks the rider vs. the venue and resolves the gaps before the day.
  • The stage plan and input list guide the setup and the sound operation.

Technical Rider: the Document that Defines the Event

The technical rider is the document issued by the artist's production that lists all the infrastructure requirements for putting on the show: sound, lighting, stage, electrical power, dressing room, and logistics. As a producer, your responsibility is to analyze each clause and decide what the event can meet, what needs negotiation, and what is technically unfeasible. Items marked as mandatory cannot simply be ignored — failing to meet them can result in cancellation, contractual penalty, or, in safety cases, an accident. Preferential items are negotiable, but they affect the quality of the delivery as perceived by the artist. A careful analysis of the rider before signing the contract is the professional producer's main risk filter.

f08dda32-5363-4765-9403-4609e9e0df99.titulo: Building the team: roles, hiring and scheduling
f08dda32-5363-4765-9403-4609e9e0df99.conteudo_rico:
To start: Building a team is essential. An event is only as good as the people who run it.

In this lesson you will learn about the roles on a production team, the hiring criteria, and how to build the work schedule that distributes people across the pre-event, during and post-event phases.

  • Technical and support roles: Sound technician, lighting technician, VJ, effects technician, security technician, transport technician, roadie, personal producer, technical producer and artistic team (band, DJ, musicians, backing acts). Each role has its own responsibility and profile.
  • Key production positions: Production manager (overall supervision and logistics coordination), artistic director (creative direction), sound engineer (studio and live mixing), road manager (tour logistics and real-time problem solving), stage technician, lighting designer, marketing coordinator, artist's personal assistant, tour support crew and local hires.
  • Hiring criteria: Select based on technical skill AND cultural fit and commitment to the project's vision. For each position, assess proven experience, specific skills (organization, communication) and, in sensitive roles, discretion and reliability. Open communication and collaboration are fundamental.
  • Local hires: For events away from home base, local professionals who know the regulations and have an established network in the city. They reduce risk and the cost of moving the crew.
  • The work schedule: A document that distributes who does what in each phase: pre-production (the week before), show day (morning, afternoon, evening/during, after) and post-production (the next day and ongoing support). It ensures every moment has someone responsible and avoids coverage gaps.

Equipment / details

  • Team and contacts spreadsheet: Name | Role | Contact. The basis for scheduling, logistics and communication. Keep it updated.
  • Work schedule (spreadsheet): Distributes roles by phase (pre, day, post). Each block with a named person in charge.
  • Vendor/crew hiring document: Records the vendor, contracted service, date/time and agreed price for each hire, along with responsibilities and general terms.

Step by step

  1. 1. List the roles the event requires: Not every event needs a VJ or a dedicated lighting designer. Size it to the scale.
  2. 2. Define criteria per position: For each role, note the technical skill + profile (reliability, communication) required.
  3. 3. Select and hire: Assess experience and fit. Formalize it with the hiring document (service, date, price).
  4. 4. Consider local hires: For events away from home base, hire local support that knows the city and its rules.
  5. 5. Build the schedule by phase: Distribute the team across pre-production, the day (morning/afternoon/during/after) and post.
  6. 6. Run the responsibilities briefing: Each person must clearly know what they do and when. Keep contingency for the unexpected.

In practice: For a show, the schedule looks like this: the week before, the production manager plans and the stage technician checks equipment; on the morning of the day, setup; in the afternoon, soundcheck and lighting; at night, sound and lighting running the show; after, teardown; the next day, review and report. Each block with a name. Without a schedule, someone always "thought it was the other person's job."

Summary

  • The team combines technical, artistic and support roles with key management positions.
  • Hire based on technical skill AND reliability and alignment with the project.
  • The schedule distributes people in charge by phase and avoids coverage gaps.

The team: production's most critical asset

Building the right team is the decision that most impacts the quality and safety of an event. Each type of event — show, corporate, congress or party — requires a different set of roles, some mandatory by law (such as a fire warden) and others operationally indispensable. The choice of hiring regime — freelance, full employee, intermittent or outsourced — affects cost, legal ties and the stability of the team. Finally, the shift schedule must respect legal and performance limits: shifts over 12h increase labor risk and compromise delivery on stage.

9fed4ee2-09df-4046-b532-63eb662c232f.titulo: Event budget: how to build the spreadsheet
9fed4ee2-09df-4046-b532-63eb662c232f.conteudo_rico:
To start: The budget is where the event proves it is viable. Without it, you produce in the dark.

Building the pre-event budget spreadsheet is a crucial step to identify and manage all costs. In this lesson you will learn the complete step-by-step process of building a budget, with formulas, contingency and validation.

  • Cost categories: List every possible category: venue rental, crew, equipment, marketing, catering, transport, decor, among others. Nothing should be left out.
  • Fixed vs. variable costs: Fixed costs are constant (renting the hall); variable costs change with the scale/number of attendees (catering per person). Distinguishing the two helps you simulate scenarios.
  • Direct vs. indirect costs: Direct costs are tied to the event (stage, artist); indirect costs include administrative expenses, insurance and fees. Forgetting indirect costs blows the budget later.
  • Contingency: Set aside a percentage of the total for unforeseen costs and last-minute changes. It's what separates the experienced producer from the optimist.
  • Fees and taxes: Include the fees and taxes applicable to the costs to avoid surprises in the final amount.
  • Spreadsheet structure: Columns: Category | Item description | Quantity | Unit cost | Total cost. Use formulas (quantity x unit cost) and subtotals per category plus a grand total.
  • Validation and updating: Review it to make sure nothing was left out, share it with stakeholders (team, client, sponsors) and update it whenever new information comes up.

Equipment / details

  • Budget spreadsheet (Google Sheets / Excel): Where the budget lives. Use SUM, multiplication and currency formatting. A summary tab with subtotals and grand total.
  • Vendor quotes: Supporting documents (emails, proposals) that back up each estimated amount. Link them to the spreadsheet.
  • Budget structure template: A template with standard categories (venue, catering, marketing, equipment, transport, decor, administrative) to reuse across events.

Step by step

  1. 1. List the cost categories: Survey all fronts of the event. Use the briefing as a base.
  2. 2. Detail the items per category: For each category, create lines with description, quantity and unit cost.
  3. 3. Estimate with real quotes: Base the amounts on market research, vendor quotes and experience. Never guess.
  4. 4. Apply formulas and subtotals: Total cost = quantity x unit. Add subtotals per category and the grand total.
  5. 5. Add contingency, indirect costs, fees and taxes: Set aside the contingency and include indirect costs, fees and taxes.
  6. 6. Review, validate and share: Check that nothing was left out, validate with stakeholders and keep the supporting document (quotes) attached.
  7. 7. Update throughout the project: Whenever something changes, update the spreadsheet to keep it accurate.

In practice: A simple example: Venue (1 hall x $5,000 = $5,000) + Catering (150 coffee breaks x $10 = $1,500) + Marketing (5,000 flyers x $0.20 = $1,000)... partial total $15,000. On top of that, you add contingency (e.g., 10% = $1,500), indirect costs, fees and taxes. The final number is what makes the event viable or not.

Summary

  • The budget lists all categories, distinguishing fixed/variable and direct/indirect.
  • Use formulas, subtotals and a grand total; estimate with real quotes.
  • Always set aside contingency and include fees/taxes so you don't overrun.

Image: Structure of an Event Budget — where the money goes (and the margin): Selling Price = Costs (fixed + variable) + Taxes + Margin (profit) + Contingency. Without contingency, any surprise eats your margin — always budget the reserve. Track the CASH FLOW: when money comes in (deposit/installments) vs when it goes out (suppliers).

Event Budget: from Spreadsheet to Selling Price

A professional budget maps all direct costs per category (production, audiovisual, infrastructure, crew and vendors) and adds a Technical Reserve — a percentage of the total cost — to cover unforeseen events such as equipment failures and overtime during setup. The selling price is not obtained by adding a percentage to the cost (markup), but by the formula Price = Total Cost ÷ (1 − Margin − Taxes), which ensures that margin and taxes are calculated on revenue, not on cost. Ignoring this detail is one of the main causes of loss in events.

641b86e1-b247-498a-beef-58c1414720a5.titulo: Financial documents: expenses, payments and contracts
641b86e1-b247-498a-beef-58c1414720a5.conteudo_rico:
To start: Money without documentation is a problem. Every dollar that comes in or goes out needs a record.

Beyond the budget, the producer keeps three operational financial documents: the expenses document, the payments document and the contracts document. In this lesson you will see the structure of each one and how to use them to maintain control and transparency.

  • Pre-event expenses document: Organizes expenses into groups (general, specific and administrative), each item with description, quantity, unit cost and total cost, with subtotals per group and a grand total. It shows where the money goes before the event.
  • Payments document: Records, per vendor, each service with: expected date, expected amount, payment date, amount paid and payment method (transfer, card, check, cash). It tracks total expected vs. total paid and keeps receipts and proofs.
  • Contracts document: For each vendor/technical crew: name, contracted service, date and time, and agreed amount. It includes contractual responsibilities, general terms and conditions and space for the organizer's signature.
  • Payment methods and proof: Each transaction can have a different method; the essential thing is to record it and keep proof. Supporting documentation (receipts, invoices) backs up control and accountability.
  • Legal guidance: When drafting contracts, always seek legal guidance to cover the legal aspects. Templates are starting points; they do not replace legal counsel.

Equipment / details

  • Expenses spreadsheet (by group): General, specific and administrative with subtotals and a grand total. Reflects the pre-event cash outflow.
  • Payments spreadsheet (expected vs. paid): Controls expected date, expected amount, date and amount paid, and method. Shows what is still pending.
  • Contracts document template: Records each vendor, service, date/time and agreed amount, with clauses and signature.
  • Receipts and proofs folder: Organized file of invoices, service receipts and proofs. The basis for accountability and closing.

Step by step

  1. 1. Build the expenses document: Group into general, specific and administrative; calculate subtotals and a grand total.
  2. 2. Create the payments tracker: For each vendor, record expected vs. paid, dates and method. Update it with each payment.
  3. 3. Formalize the contracts: For each hire, record the service, date/time and agreed amount, with terms and signature.
  4. 4. Keep all proofs: A receipt, invoice or transfer proof for each payment. Without proof, there was no payment (for control purposes).
  5. 5. Keep clear communication with vendors: Any change to service, date or amount must be agreed in writing between the parties.
  6. 6. Seek legal guidance on contracts: Validate clauses with counsel before signing.

In practice: You close catering for $1,500, payment expected on the 10th via transfer. In the payments document, the line reads: expected $1,500 / paid $1,500 on the 10th / transfer, with the proof attached in the folder. At the post-event closing, you just add up the "amount paid" to know exactly how much went out.

Summary

  • Expenses, payments and contracts are the three operational financial documents.
  • Payments track expected vs. paid, date and method, with proof kept on file.
  • Templates are a starting point: contracts require legal guidance.

Image: Financial Settlement — Revenue − Costs = Result: Revenue (box office, sponsorship, bar, fees) − Costs (suppliers, crew, venue, taxes) = Result (profit or loss). Reconcile everything with invoices and receipts. The post-event report shows what worked and what to improve.

Financial documents: expenses, payments and contracts

In event production, every financial transaction needs proper documentary backing: electronic invoices (NF-e), tax payment slips (DARF), receipts with taxpayer IDs and advance-payment proofs must be classified in the appropriate accounting category to ensure tax compliance. Payments should only be released when documentation is complete and the service has already been rendered or there is contractual guarantee — paying without an invoice exposes the company to penalties and makes it impossible to claim tax credit. Service contracts must mandatorily contain clauses for cancellation penalties, liability for damages, withholding of service tax at source and the requirement of an invoice before any disbursement, protecting the producer in case of default or termination.

4fbaf91c-4e38-45ae-868e-ca005b97d8ff.titulo: Securing the logistics and overseeing the setup
4fbaf91c-4e38-45ae-868e-ca005b97d8ff.conteudo_rico:
To start: On event day, the producer stops planning and starts making things happen. Logistics and setup are the first test.

Securing the logistics is fundamental to the success of the production. In this lesson you will see the producer's responsibilities in the prior logistics planning and, above all, in overseeing the setup on the day, a critical phase that determines whether the event starts off well.

  • Prior logistics planning: Confirm the availability and suitability of the venue and the date/time; collaborate with the technical crew to meet the rider's requirements; and organize transport and lodging for the crew and equipment with advance booking.
  • Arrival coordination: Ensure that technical crew, artists and everyone involved arrive according to the schedule, keeping open communication to handle delays or transport problems.
  • Presence on site from the start of setup: The producer must be present BEFORE setup begins to supervise from the start, greet and coordinate the technical crew, making sure everyone understands their roles.
  • Coordination with the technical crew: Review the technical plan with the crew, be available to clarify questions and closely monitor the setup of stage, sound, lighting and other equipment, ensuring the schedule is met.
  • Equipment logistics and testing: Verify that all equipment is present and working, anticipate problems with spare parts and run initial tests to avoid surprises during the event.
  • Backstage environment: Supervise the organization of the backstage so it is functional and comfortable, with food and drinks available, coordinating proper lighting and catering with the right departments.
  • Final check: After setup, do an inspection of the venue to ensure everything is as planned and in line with expectations, before the event begins.

Equipment / details

  • Logistics plan and setup schedule: The day's guiding documents. The setup schedule details what goes up and when (stage, sound, lighting).
  • Two-way radio: Continuous communication between production and technical crew during setup. Speeds up problem solving.
  • Equipment / spare parts checklist: Ensures everything has arrived and works, and anticipates failures with backup items.

Step by step

  1. 1. Confirm venue, transport and lodging beforehand: Advance bookings and validated suitability of the space.
  2. 2. Be on site before setup begins: Arrive before the technical crew to coordinate from the start.
  3. 3. Review the technical plan with the crew: Make sure everyone understands the specifications and their roles.
  4. 4. Monitor the setup against the schedule: Track stage, sound and lighting and enforce the deadlines. Act if it falls behind.
  5. 5. Run the initial tests: Test equipment early so problems show up with time to solve them.
  6. 6. Organize the backstage and do the final inspection: Functional dressing room, catering and lighting; then inspect everything before opening.

In practice: The producer arrives at 7:00 AM, before the truck. Greets the crew, reviews the setup schedule, follows the stage and sound going up, notices a monitor won't turn on and — because there was a spare part and time — solves it before the soundcheck. When the artist arrives, everything is tested. That anticipation is the job.

Summary

  • Prior logistics: confirm venue, transport, lodging and technical requirements.
  • The producer arrives before setup and coordinates the crew from the start.
  • Initial tests and spare parts make problems show up with time to solve them.

Logistics and Setup Oversight

Securing an event's logistics begins before the first truck arrives: rider confirmation, access credentials, invoice verification and assigning people in charge of each point. During setup, the producer must follow each front (structure, audio, video, security and soundcheck) checking critical items that, if ignored, can halt the show or create real risk. Effective oversight combines a structured checklist, permanent communication between teams (radios/headsets) and the recording of incidents with documented resolution — ensuring that doors open with safety and technical quality.

3fb82069-5851-4045-bdd9-b48967830086.conteudo_rico:
To start: With the event underway, the producer is the central point. Everything runs through them and they keep everything on track.

Managing execution is the stage where the producer centralizes communications, tracks the schedule in real time and solves problems under pressure. In this lesson you will see the responsibilities during the show and how to keep the operation under control.

  • Overall coordination: Act as the central point for all communications during the event, with a clear and effective line to the entire crew, and supervise all activities to ensure they happen as planned.
  • Real-time schedule tracking: Regularly monitor the schedule, be ready to adjust it as needed and promptly communicate any change to technical crew, artists and collaborators.
  • Problem solving: Make quick and effective decisions for emerging problems, collaborating with the crew and keeping transparent communication with clear guidance.
  • Vendor and partner relations: Oversee compliance with contracts and be prepared to mediate conflicts between parties, seeking solutions that keep a positive and professional environment.
  • People management: Offer ongoing support to the crew, meet needs and provide additional resources; recognize achievements and proactively handle concerns, keeping morale up.
  • Ongoing evaluation and financial management: Give immediate feedback to the crew, be open to suggestions, and monitor costs during the event to keep them within budget, deciding quickly when needed.
  • Composure under pressure: Stay calm and composed, conveying confidence to the crew. The producer is the operation's emotional thermometer: if they lose control, the crew loses it too.

Equipment / details

  • Radio / communication group: Keeps the producer as the central point. Decisions and changes circulate here in real time.
  • Day's schedule (printed and digital): A constant reference to check whether the event is following the plan and to adjust when needed.
  • Contingency plan: A "what to do if" list (technical failure, delay, emergency) prepared beforehand. Speeds up decisions under pressure.

Step by step

  1. 1. Centralize communication: Be the point through which information and decisions pass. Avoid parallel, contradictory orders.
  2. 2. Monitor the schedule in real time: Compare what is happening with the plan and adjust when needed.
  3. 3. Communicate changes immediately: Every change to timing or flow goes to those involved right away.
  4. 4. Decide quickly on the unexpected: Collaborate with the crew, choose the practical solution and give clear guidance.
  5. 5. Support and motivate the crew: Provide support, recognize the effort and keep the atmosphere professional under pressure.
  6. 6. Control the day's costs: Monitor extra spending and decide with judgment so as not to overrun the budget.

In practice: The show is running 20 minutes late because the opening band ran long. The producer recalculates the schedule on the spot, alerts lighting, sound and the road manager over the radio, communicates the change to the bar and security for the crowd flow, and stays calm so the crew doesn't panic. In 5 minutes, the event is back under control.

Summary

  • During execution, the producer centralizes communication and supervises everything.
  • Monitoring the schedule in real time and communicating changes right away is essential.
  • Calm and a contingency plan turn the unexpected into quick decisions.

Managing the Event's Execution

Execution is the moment when planning turns into reality: the producer must monitor the rundown, coordinate teams over the radio and make quick decisions when the unexpected arises. Each work front — audio, video, lighting, catering, security and reception — must be tracked in real time, and any deviation requires clear communication and immediate action. The ability to prioritize, delegate and resolve incidents without losing control of the overall flow is what sets a full-level producer apart. Client satisfaction is directly tied to the quality of the decisions made during execution.

b2106b82-473d-4f80-a279-a1fc9994d6d3.conteudo_rico:
To begin: The event is only over when the venue is clean, the accounts are closed, and the equipment is returned. Not before.

The post-event phase begins with coordinating the load-out and the financial closing. In this lesson you will see how to wind down the operation in an organized, safe way with the accounts in order, a condition for the health of your next productions.

  • Load-out coordination: Assign specific tasks to the team, supervise from start to finish, and give clear instructions on how to dismantle and pack each piece of equipment, checking for any damage during the process.
  • Transport and returns logistics: Communicate with transport suppliers to secure vehicles, list and prepare everything for loading, and coordinate the return of rented or borrowed equipment within the deadlines.
  • Stage and backstage teardown: Dismantle the stage safely and to the correct standards, collect materials (barricades, fencing), tidy up the backstage, and return personal belongings to artists and crew.
  • Waste disposal and sustainability: Plan the proper disposal of waste, coordinate with removal services, and promote sustainable practices (recycling), leaving the venue clean and in order.
  • Review of expenses and revenue: Review all expenses and revenue, check that they match the contracts, and ensure complete documentation (invoices, receipts, statements).
  • Accounting, payments, and collections: Update the accounting records, perform the bank reconciliation, finalize pending payments to suppliers/artists/crew, and follow up on receivables (sponsorships, tickets).
  • Financial reports and filing: Prepare income statements, compare the actuals with the initial budget, analyze variances, and file all documents in an organized way for the required period.

Equipment / details

  • Load-out checklist: A list of tasks and responsible people to close out in an organized way without losing equipment.
  • Returns tracking: A spreadsheet of what was rented/borrowed, the deadline, and the return status. Prevents being charged for an item that was not returned.
  • Financial closing spreadsheet: Final expenses and revenue, actual vs. budgeted, with bank reconciliation and variances.
  • Financial documents folder: Invoices, receipts, statements, and proofs filed for the legally required period.

Step by step

  1. 1. Distribute the load-out tasks: Each person with a clear responsibility; supervise from start to finish.
  2. 2. Check equipment and organize the returns: Inspect for damage, pack correctly, and coordinate the returns within the deadline.
  3. 3. Collect belongings and leave the venue in order: Return personal belongings, dispose of waste, and hand over the venue clean.
  4. 4. Review expenses and revenue: Check everything against the contracts and gather the complete documentation.
  5. 5. Finalize payments and collections: Settle outstanding items and follow up on receivables; perform the bank reconciliation.
  6. 6. Compare actual vs. budgeted and file: Analyze variances, prepare the financial report, and file the documents.

In practice: After the show, the load-out is distributed: the tech crew dismantles sound and lighting, roadies pack the backline, and support staff clear the backstage. The returns tracking ensures the 2 rented monitor wedges come back on time. On the financial side, the producer adds up the 'amount paid', compares it with the budget (e.g., spent R$26,000 out of the R$27,900 planned) and closes the accounts with the proofs filed.

Summary

  • The load-out is coordinated with tasks, returns, and a clean venue.
  • The financial closing reviews expenses/revenue, reconciles, and compares with the budget.
  • Filing proofs in an organized way sustains your next productions.

Load-out and Post-Event Financial Closing

The load-out phase is as critical as the load-in: CRITICAL items — such as confirming the closeout with the client, safely powering down the electrical system, and a signed inspection of the venue — must be completed before closing in order to avoid liability disputes and losses. The financial closing consists of comparing gross revenue against all direct costs (crew, equipment, transport, taxes) and recording occurrences with a monetary impact, arriving at the net result and the event's margin. A margin below 15% indicates a risk zone; above 30% is a benchmark of financial health to use as a model in future proposals.

3311b215-1ced-43f8-8ba0-c0fb07643bf2.conteudo_rico:
To start: The post-event report turns experience into learning. It's what makes the next event better than this one.

Preparing post-event reports is an essential practice to evaluate performance, identify improvements and generate insights for future productions. In this lesson you will learn the complete structure of the report and how to run it.

  • Introduction and objectives: An executive summary with the main highlights, the report's purpose, and a review of the objectives set before the event, evaluating how far they were achieved.
  • Planning, attendance and audience: An analysis of the planning's effectiveness (logistics, schedule, crew), total number of attendees compared to expectations, and the audience's demographic profile (age range, location).
  • Audience evaluation: Results of satisfaction surveys and feedback, with positive and negative points, and an analysis of social media interactions (posts, shares, mentions).
  • Artistic performance and operations: An evaluation of the artists (audience response, quality, challenges) and of the efficiency of the logistics operations (setup, teardown, equipment), pointing out improvements.
  • Finances and budget: A comparison of actual costs with the budget, identifying deviations and reasons, and a presentation of revenue (tickets, sponsorships), evaluating fundraising.
  • Lessons learned and recommendations: The main lessons from the event, corrective and preventive actions, and specific recommendations for future productions, considering feedback from crew, artists and audience.
  • Acknowledgments, appendices and conclusion: Recognition of those involved, supporting appendices (photos, financial charts, media reports) and a final summary on the event's success and takeaways.

Equipment / details

  • Post-event report template: A document structured in the sections above. Reuse it as a template. Use charts and visualizations to make it easier to read.
  • Satisfaction survey (Google Forms): Collects audience feedback in a structured way and generates data for the report automatically.
  • Event records folder: Photos, videos, social media screenshots and financial charts that become appendices and evidence.

Step by step

  1. 1. Open with an executive summary and objectives: Main highlights and a review of the objectives set before the event.
  2. 2. Evaluate planning, audience and satisfaction: Planning effectiveness, audience number and profile and survey results.
  3. 3. Analyze artistic performance and operations: Audience response to the artists and logistics efficiency, with improvements.
  4. 4. Compare finances with the budget: Actual vs. projected costs, revenue and sponsorships, with deviations analyzed.
  5. 5. Record lessons and recommendations: What worked, what to improve and actions for the next events.
  6. 6. Attach evidence and share: Photos, charts and media reports; distribute to the crew and stakeholders.

In practice: After the festival, the report shows: a target of 800 people, 950 came (above expectations); satisfaction of 4.3/5; projected budget $80k, spent $78k; lesson learned: the entrance line was stuck for 30 min, recommendation to open one more gate. At the next event, that learning avoids the same mistake. That's how production evolves.

Summary

  • The post-event report evaluates objectives, audience, artistic performance, operations and finances.
  • Lessons learned and recommendations feed your next productions.
  • Charts, photos and satisfaction data give the report evidence.

Why the post-event report defines the producer's career

The post-event report is the document that turns experience into institutional learning. It records what was planned versus what happened, identifies failures and successes, and delivers to the client concrete evidence of the work done. Well-structured reports include an executive summary, budget comparison, schedule with deviations, incident log and recommendations for the next edition. Producers who document correctly build a portfolio, protect themselves legally and improve with each event — while those who don't document repeat the same mistakes indefinitely.

a697a3a7-5a0e-4a4c-bae8-809b00b15057.conteudo_rico:
To begin: A producer is only as good as the organization of their files. A lost document means a delayed decision.

Organizing and storing documents efficiently is crucial for managing the project and keeping information accessible. In this lesson you will learn the folder structure, file naming, the use of cloud storage and software, and the practices for communicating documentation to the team.

  • Digital folder structure: One main folder per artist/project, with standardized subfolders: 01_Contracts_and_Agreements, 02_Technical_Rider_and_Logistics, 03_Budget_and_Finance, 04_Marketing_and_Promotion, 05_Contacts_and_Lists, 06_Legal_Documentation, 07_Production_Art_and_Design, 08_Records_of_Past_Events, 09_Travel_Logistics.
  • Structure by event: For the event producer, an 'Events' folder with subfolders per event named with the date (e.g., 2025-05-12_Festival_Planeta_Beat), containing Artists, Images, Team and Documents. Add to that a 'Registered_Artists' folder (riders, press kit, restricted-access documents) and a 'Models and Templates' folder.
  • File naming: A consistent standard: DocumentType_EventName-Artist_Date.extension (e.g., Contract_ShowSP_ArtistXYZ_2025-06-15.pdf). Always use the date in the format YYYY-MM-DD and version numbers to distinguish updates.
  • Cloud storage: Google Drive (integration with Workspace), Dropbox (version control) or OneDrive (Office 365). Create shared folders with permissions by role, use passwords and two-factor authentication, and back up regularly.
  • Management software: Trello (schedules and task breakdown), Asana (larger teams, email integration), Monday (visual tracking of stages) and Notion (documents and databases in the same environment). Google Docs/Teams for online collaboration.
  • Communicating the documentation: Centralize the documents, keep an organized structure and share with permissions. Use automatic notifications, a shared calendar, informative emails, regular meetings, checklists and instant messaging tools (Slack, Teams). Establish quick-response protocols for last-minute changes.
  • Documentation maintenance checklist: Periodic routines with an owner: review contracts (monthly), organize files by date (weekly), backup (weekly), audit of folders and permissions (quarterly), feedback meeting (twice a year).

Equipment / details

  • Google Drive / Dropbox / OneDrive: Central cloud storage with folders shared by role and automatic backup. Use colors and emojis in folder names for quick identification.
  • Trello / Asana / Monday / Notion: Management of tasks and production stages. Trello for simple boards; Notion to combine documents and databases.
  • Google Forms: Collects artist data (registration form) and organizes it directly into a spreadsheet on Drive. Great for standardizing the input of information.
  • Slack / Microsoft Teams: Instant communication with specific channels per area (logistics, marketing) for quick alignments.

Step by step

  1. 1. Create the main folder and the standard subfolders: In Drive, set up the artist/project or event folder with the numbered subfolders.
  2. 2. Define the naming standard: Adopt DocumentType_Event-Artist_YYYY-MM-DD and versions. Apply it to ALL files.
  3. 3. Adjust permissions and security: Share by role, restrict sensitive folders (finance, personal documents) and enable two-factor.
  4. 4. Choose the management software: Pick Trello/Asana/Monday/Notion according to team size and keep the tasks there.
  5. 5. Centralize and communicate: Use a shared calendar, notifications and meetings so the team always knows where each document is.
  6. 6. Keep up the maintenance routine: Follow the checklist (review, backup, audit, feedback) with defined owners.

In practice: It is 2 days before the show and the lighting tech asks for the rider. In an organized production, they go into 02_Technical_Rider_and_Logistics, find 'Technical_Rider_EventX_2025-06-15.pdf' in the right version and solve it on their own in 30 seconds. In a messy production, they call the producer in the middle of the load-in. Digital organization is what gives you time on the busiest day.

Summary

  • Standardized folders and consistent naming (date YYYY-MM-DD, version) make everything findable.
  • Cloud with permissions by role, two-factor and backup protects the documentation.
  • Centralizing and communicating the documentation, with a maintenance routine, sustains the operation.

Documentation, Digital Organization and Communication

An efficient event producer structures their operation around three pillars: documentation (contracts, invoices, riders and reports at the right time), digital organization (each file in its folder, with a standardized name and clear versioning) and professional communication (the right channel, appropriate tone and a traceable record). Failures in these pillars generate rework, contractual conflicts and loss of credibility with clients and suppliers. In this simulator, you practice the real decisions that define a production company's level of documentation maturity.

2c094cbd-3318-4564-aeeb-0526e76eec29.titulo: Jargon, the senior producer and career vision
2c094cbd-3318-4564-aeeb-0526e76eec29.conteudo_rico:
To begin: Mastering the language of backstage and understanding what makes a senior producer is what completes your training.

This final lesson brings together the practical vocabulary of backstage and the profile of the senior producer, stitching together everything you have learned into a career vision: from briefing to report, from beginner to the professional who leads complex productions.

  • Backstage jargon: 'It's down to the wire' = the time deadline is about to be blown, it's tight. 'Let's wrap it up' = let's go, let's close/move on. Backstage language is fast and direct; understanding it avoids noise in the operation.
  • The senior producer: experience and leadership: A highly experienced professional who leads and oversees all planning, execution and evaluation of events. They coordinate teams, making sure each member understands their responsibilities and contributes to success.
  • Strategic planning and budget management: Develops strategies considering objectives, budgets, logistics, marketing and audience experience, and oversees the drafting and management of budgets with efficient use of resources.
  • Relationships and negotiation: Interacts with clients, sponsors and partners, building solid relationships, and negotiates contracts with suppliers, artists and collaborators seeking fair and advantageous deals.
  • Risk management and decision-making: Proactively identifies and manages risks that may affect the event and makes quick, strategic decisions to solve problems or adjust plans during the operation.
  • Innovation, mentoring and ethics: Seeks creativity for memorable experiences, guides less experienced members through mentoring and development, and upholds high ethical and professional standards in all interactions.
  • The producer's journey: From the briefing to the post-event report, every document and phase you have learned connects. The complete producer masters pre, during and post, and matures into the senior level with experience, client focus and strategic vision.

Equipment / details

  • Personal jargon glossary: Write down the terms you hear backstage. Every scene/region has its own; mastering the language speeds up integration into the team.
  • Event portfolio: Gather reports, photos and feedback from the events you have produced. It is what proves experience and supports the growth toward senior.

Step by step

  1. 1. Learn the language of your field: Listen to and record the jargon of your scene. Use it naturally in the operation.
  2. 2. Map your senior-level competencies: Compare yourself to the senior profile (strategy, negotiation, risk, mentoring) and see where to grow.
  3. 3. Build relationships: Take care of clients, sponsors and suppliers. Trust is your greatest career asset.
  4. 4. Document your track record: Keep reports and results from each event in your portfolio.
  5. 5. Practice mentoring and ethics: Teach those who are starting out and uphold ethical standards; that is what builds a senior reputation.

In practice: You have already done the full cycle: briefing, licenses, rider, schedule, budget, team, execution and report. When someone says 'it's down to the wire, let's wrap it up', you understand and act. With several documented events in your portfolio, solid relationships and increasingly strategic decisions, you move from mid-level producer to senior producer.

Summary

  • Jargon speeds up communication backstage ('down to the wire', 'wrap it up').
  • The senior producer combines experience, strategy, negotiation, risk management and mentoring.
  • From briefing to report, mastering pre, during and post makes the complete producer.

Language, Decision-Making and Career in Event Production

Technical vocabulary — rider, FOH, hold, bumper in, rundown, backline — is not mere jargon: it is the language that distinguishes the assistant from the senior and determines whether suppliers and clients trust you. Mastering these terms is the first step to operating with authority in the market.

Beyond vocabulary, a career vision in production is built through progressive responsibility and documenting mistakes, not through luck or time in the market. Each stage — assistant, coordinator, executive producer, senior, director — requires specific competencies and the ability to make correct technical decisions under pressure, communicating with clarity and advance notice.

0785fc6b-4b6a-4d24-9784-e514e35c20a7.titulo: Professional Budgeting: Cost Structure, Margin and Contingency
0785fc6b-4b6a-4d24-9784-e514e35c20a7.conteudo_rico:
To begin: An event doesn't fail for lack of talent — it fails because of a poorly built budget. Before thinking about lighting, sound or line-up, you need to know exactly where every dollar goes.

In this lesson you will learn to build an event budget like a real company: separating fixed from variable costs, calculating the break-even point, building in margin and contingency, and projecting cash flow so you don't go into the red before the day of the show. Let's leave 'guesswork' behind and get into the spreadsheet.

  • Fixed costs: These are the ones that don't vary with the size of the audience within a range: venue rental, artist fee, stage structure, sound and lighting hired per day, permanent technical crew, insurance. You pay the same amount with 200 or 800 people. Add these up first because they set the floor of expense.
  • Variable costs: They grow with the audience or with the operation: cups, ice, bar supplies, box-office commission per ticket sold, card/gateway fee, wristbands, extra security by occupancy, cleaning in proportion. Always calculate per unit (cost per person) to project scenarios.
  • Break-even point: It is how many tickets (or what revenue) you need to cover all costs. Practical formula: Fixed Costs ÷ (ticket price − variable cost per person). Below that you make a loss; above it, profit. Knowing this number before opening sales is non-negotiable.
  • Profit margin: It is what's left after paying for everything, expressed as a % of revenue. In event production, healthy margins usually range between 15% and 30% depending on the risk. Margin is not 'leftover that appears' — you define it beforehand, adding it to the cost to arrive at the price.
  • Contingency (technical reserve): A reserve of 5% to 15% of the total budget for the unexpected: rain, blown equipment, overtime, a supplier who failed. It is not fat, it is risk management. Events without contingency turn any surprise into a direct loss.
  • Cash flow: It is the calendar of inflows and outflows over time. You pay supplier deposits weeks in advance, but you only receive the box-office revenue in the post-event settlement. Mapping this avoids the classic scenario: an event profitable on paper, but with no money on hand to pay today's bill.
  • Forms of charging and receiving: Box office by platform (Sympla, Shotgun, Ingresse) with settlement a few days later; sales at the door (cash/Pix/card reader); sponsorship via contract and invoice; barter. Each has a different term and fee — the payment term directly impacts the cash flow.
  • Invoicing and formalization: To receive from sponsors, venues and production companies you need to issue an invoice — as an MEI, ME or production company. A service invoice (NFS-e) is municipal. Working formally gives access to larger contracts, avoids tax problems and professionalizes your image. Taxation varies by regime (e.g., Simples Nacional).

Equipment / details

  • Budget spreadsheet (Excel/Google Sheets): Master document with tabs for fixed costs, variable costs, revenue and scenarios. Use formulas so the break-even recalculates itself when you change price or audience.
  • Cash-flow model: A month-by-month (or week-by-week) table with the expected date of each inflow and outflow, showing the accumulated balance to identify when cash goes negative.
  • NF-e/NFS-e issuer: The city hall system or an accounting platform to issue invoices for the services provided and for sponsorship/fee receipts.

Step by step

  1. List all fixed costs: Gather artist fee, venue, structure, crew and insurance. Ask suppliers for quotes and use the real figure, not one estimated off the top of your head.
  2. Calculate variable costs per person: Add up supplies, fees and commissions and divide by the estimated audience to get the unit cost.
  3. Set the ticket price and find the break-even: Apply Fixed Costs ÷ (price − variable cost per person) to know how many tickets cover everything.
  4. Add margin and contingency: Add the desired margin (e.g., 20%) and the reserve (e.g., 10%) to the total cost before setting the final price.
  5. Build the cash flow over time: Place each payment and receipt on its real date and check that the balance never goes negative; if it does, negotiate terms or bring revenue forward.

In practice: You are going to produce a party for 400 people. Fixed costs add up to R$ 32,000 (venue, headliner DJ, sound/lighting, crew). Variable cost per person is R$ 15 (cup, box-office fee, proportional security). Ticket at R$ 80. Break-even: 32,000 ÷ (80 − 15) = ~493 tickets — meaning that at 400 people you would have a LOSS. Here you decide: raise the price, cut a fixed cost, increase the audience target or seek sponsorship to cover the gap. It is this calculation, done BEFORE, that saves the event.

Summary

  • Fixed cost is the floor; variable cost scales with the audience — separate them to project scenarios.
  • The break-even point tells you the minimum to avoid a loss; margin and contingency are added BEFORE the price.
  • An event profitable on paper can fail because of cash flow: map when money comes in and goes out.

Professional Budgeting: Cost Structure, Margin and Contingency

A professional event budget is made up of direct costs (production, suppliers, rental), indirect costs (logistics, accommodation, fees) and a contingency reserve — an amount set aside to cover the unexpected without compromising the result. On top of that total, the profit margin and tax charges are applied. The difference between the minimum viable price and the amount proposed to the client defines the project's financial health: a proposal below the floor generates a loss; a margin below 15% makes the event vulnerable to any unplanned surprise.

8f16af9b-7415-4df0-9b4a-0a4642a9dd99.titulo: Contracts and Legal: Clauses, Copyright (ECAD) and Insurance
8f16af9b-7415-4df0-9b4a-0a4642a9dd99.conteudo_rico:
To begin: When you improvise, everything seems to go fine — until someone doesn't honor what was agreed. A contract is not distrust: it is what turns a promise into an enforceable obligation.

This lesson gives you the foundation to negotiate and read event contracts without being a lawyer — understanding essential clauses, civil liability, copyright and ECAD, and insurance. The goal is to make you able to guide and hold your legal advisor accountable, not to replace them. On specific figures and rules, always confirm with a lawyer.

  • Why always a contract: A contract defines the object, deadlines, amounts, obligations and penalties in an enforceable way. Without it, any breach becomes 'his word against hers'. For artist, supplier, venue and sponsor, the contract protects both sides and provides predictability.
